Python automation: How would you use the: Use the official kubernetes Python client to list pods with CoreV1Api().list_pod_for_all_namespaces(), then inspect status.container_statuses for waiting.reason values like CrashLoopBackOff and ImagePullBackOff, status.phase == 'Pending' for scheduling issues, and status.container_statuses[].restart_count above a threshold for flapping pods. Group results by namespace and reason, and export a CSV or Slack summary so on-call engineers can triage quickly without running kubectl by hand across many clusters.
Python error handling: How would you des: Wrap outbound calls with a retry decorator (e.g. tenacity) using exponential backoff with jitter, capped retry count, and a per-call timeout so one hung request cannot block the whole run. Only retry on transient errors (429, 5xx, connection resets), never on 4xx validation errors. Make operations idempotent by using deterministic request keys or checking current state before mutating, so a retried call after a partial failure does not double-apply changes.
Python logging: How would you implement: Use the standard logging module with a JSON formatter so every log line is machine-parseable, and attach a correlation/request ID via a contextvar or logging filter so all log lines for one operation can be grepped together. Emit counters and timers (e.g. via prometheus_client or OpenTelemetry) for calls made, retries, failures, and duration, and ship logs to Cloud Logging or an ELK stack so incidents can be traced end to end.
Python data parsing: How would you parse: Stream large files instead of loading them fully into memory - use ijson for large JSON, csv.reader/DictReader with a generator for CSV, and line-by-line iteration for logs. Validate and coerce types defensively so one malformed record does not crash the whole parse, and aggregate counts, top error signatures, and time ranges into a structured summary object that can be rendered as a short incident report.
Python packaging: How would you package,: Package with pyproject.toml and setuptools/hatch, pin dependencies with a lockfile, and use semantic versioning tied to git tags so consumers know what changed. Publish to an internal PyPI-compatible registry (Artifact Registry Python repo), generate docs from docstrings, and gate releases behind CI that runs lint, type checks, and tests before publishing.
Python security: How would you prevent s: Never pass secrets as CLI arguments (visible in process listings and shell history); read them from a secret manager or masked environment variables instead. Scrub log formatters and exception handlers to redact known secret patterns, avoid printing full config/env dumps, and configure CI to mask secret values in job output so a stack trace or debug print cannot exfiltrate credentials.
Python code review: What would you check: Check for a dry-run mode, explicit confirmation on destructive actions, proper error handling and retries, least-privilege credentials, and no hardcoded secrets or environment-specific values. Verify there are unit tests covering failure paths, structured logging for auditability, and that the script is idempotent so a re-run after a partial failure is safe.
Python interview coding: Write the appro: Compute the success rate as (total_requests - failed_requests) / total_requests, compare it against the SLO target to get compliance, and calculate the error budget as (1 - slo_target) * total_requests; remaining budget is error_budget - failed_requests. Guard against division by zero when total_requests is 0, and return both the raw numbers and a percentage so the caller can decide whether to gate a release.
Python type hints: How would you use typ: Add type hints to function signatures and use dataclasses (or Pydantic models) for structured config and API payloads instead of loose dicts, so typos and shape mismatches are caught before runtime. Run mypy in CI in strict mode on critical modules to catch None-handling bugs and incorrect API usage early, which matters most for scripts that touch production infrastructure.
Python decorators: How would you write a: Write a decorator using functools.wraps that times the call with time.perf_counter, wraps the invocation in a retry loop with exponential backoff on transient exceptions, and logs a structured entry with function name, duration, attempt count, and outcome. Keep the decorator generic by accepting the wrapped function's args/kwargs transparently so it composes with any automation function without changing its signature.
Python context managers: How would you i: Implement __enter__ to acquire a lock (e.g. via Cloud Storage object generation preconditions, Firestore transaction, or a Redis SETNX with TTL) and raise if it cannot be obtained within a timeout, and __implement __exit__ to always release the lock even if the operation raised, using a try/finally internally. Add a lease TTL and heartbeat so a crashed process does not hold the lock forever and block future runs.
Python CLI design: How would you design: Use Click for subcommands and typed options, add a global --dry-run flag that logs intended actions without executing them, and an --env flag validated against an allowlist (dev/staging/prod) to prevent typos from targeting the wrong environment. For destructive commands, require an explicit --yes flag or an interactive confirmation prompt that echoes back what will be changed before proceeding.
Python GCP client patterns: How would yo: Use the client library's built-in pager (e.g. list_next or the iterator returned by list()) rather than manually tracking page tokens, and wrap calls in retry logic that backs off on 429/RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED responses using the Retry-After hint when present. Batch or throttle concurrent requests to stay under per-project quotas, and checkpoint progress so a long-running inventory job can resume instead of restarting from page one.
Python dependency management: How would: Declare direct dependencies in pyproject.toml and generate a fully pinned lockfile (poetry.lock or requirements.txt via pip-compile) so every install is reproducible across machines and CI. Run a scheduled job to check for outdated or vulnerable pinned versions, and rebuild the lockfile deliberately rather than letting versions drift silently between developer machines.
Python performance: A Python automation: Profile first with cProfile or py-spy to confirm whether the bottleneck is I/O wait (network calls) or CPU-bound work, since the fix differs. For I/O-bound API calls, use asyncio with an async client (or a thread pool, since most GCP calls release the GIL during I/O) to run requests concurrently; reserve multiprocessing for genuinely CPU-bound post-processing like parsing or transforming large result sets.
Python mocking: How would you mock GCP a: Use unittest.mock or pytest-mock to patch the client methods and return canned responses or raise the actual exception types (google.api_core.exceptions.ResourceExhausted, PermissionDenied, DeadlineExceeded) so tests exercise the real except branches. For the Kubernetes client, mock the ApiClient or use a fake client library, and write one test per failure mode to confirm retries, backoff, and error messages behave as intended without touching a real cluster.
